title: Machinist
metaTitle: Machinist Resume: Sample Bullets & Skills Guide
metaDescription: Land your next machinist role. See strong resume bullets, top skills, and ATS keywords hiring managers actually search for.
h1: Machinist Resume: Template, Skills & Sample Bullets
intro: Your machinist resume needs to prove you can read blueprints, run tight tolerances, and keep equipment running safely. We'll show you how to translate hands-on shop floor wins into bullets that catch a hiring manager's eye—and pass their ATS scan.
audience: Trade school grads, apprenticeship completers, and experienced machinists looking to move into supervisory or specialized CNC roles.

topSkills.name: CNC Programming & Operation
topSkills.why: Most modern shops require CNC machine proficiency; it's often the first filter on job postings and ATS systems.

topSkills.name: Blueprint Reading & Interpretation
topSkills.why: Hiring managers need confidence you can translate technical drawings into finished parts without rework.

topSkills.name: Precision Measurement & Tolerances
topSkills.why: Ability to work within spec (micrometers, thousandths) separates reliable machinists from average ones.

topSkills.name: Manual Lathe & Milling Machine Operation
topSkills.why: Even in CNC-heavy shops, manual machine skill proves adaptability and foundational expertise.

topSkills.name: CAD Software (SolidWorks, AutoCAD)
topSkills.why: Design-to-production fluency makes you valuable for prototyping, tooling, and custom jobs.

topSkills.name: Tool & Die Making
topSkills.why: Specialized skill that commands higher wages and opens doors to advanced manufacturing roles.

topSkills.name: Quality Control & Inspection
topSkills.why: Shows you catch defects before they ship; critical for customers and regulatory compliance.

topSkills.name: Equipment Troubleshooting & Maintenance
topSkills.why: Downtime costs money; machinists who prevent or quickly fix issues are always in demand.

topSkills.name: OSHA Safety & Shop Floor Compliance
topSkills.why: Non-negotiable in regulated manufacturing; many ATS systems flag for safety certifications.

sampleBullets.weak: Operated CNC machines and performed quality checks on parts.
sampleBullets.strong: Programmed and operated CNC mills and lathes to produce 500+ precision parts monthly within ±0.001" tolerances; reduced scrap rate by 12% through proactive tool monitoring and offset adjustments.
sampleBullets.lesson: Specific numbers (part volume, tolerance specs, measurable improvement) replace vague language and prove impact.

sampleBullets.weak: Read blueprints and made parts according to specifications.
sampleBullets.strong: Interpreted complex multi-view blueprints and GD&T callouts to machine aerospace-grade aluminum and stainless components; zero rework orders in 18-month period across 200+ jobs.
sampleBullets.lesson: Naming the industry (aerospace), material, and a quality metric (zero rework) shows you understand what matters to that shop.

sampleBullets.weak: Maintained machines and kept the shop running smoothly.
sampleBullets.strong: Diagnosed and resolved spindle runout, coolant flow, and electrical issues on three lathe-mill centers; average machine uptime improved to 96%, saving an estimated 40+ hours of downtime per quarter.
sampleBullets.lesson: Concrete troubleshooting examples and uptime percentage prove you're a problem-solver, not just an operator.

commonMistakes.mistake: Listing only job titles without proof of skill depth
commonMistakes.fix: Always pair a machine type (CNC 4-axis, manual lathe, VMC) with a measurable outcome—parts produced, tolerances held, or time saved.
Add a dedicated section for any trade certifications, apprenticeships, tool-and-die licenses, or CNC programming courses; ATS systems often filter by these.

commonMistakes.mistake: Focusing on general 'teamwork' instead of technical collaboration
commonMistakes.fix: Say 'Coordinated with CAD design team to refine toolpaths, reducing machining time by 15%' instead of 'Worked well with others.'
Explicitly mention any OSHA training, lockout/tagout (LOTO) proficiency, or safety audits you've led—shops prioritize this.

commonMistakes.mistake: Not listing specific machines, materials, or industries you've worked with
commonMistakes.fix: Include exact machine models (Haas VF-4, Okuma Lathe), materials (titanium, cast iron, composites), and industries (aerospace, automotive, medical device) so hiring managers instantly see relevance.

structureTips: Lead with a 'Technical Skills' or 'Core Competencies' section that mirrors the job posting—CNC programming languages, machine types, CAD software, and certifications should appear in the first third of your resume.
In the work history, front-load your most recent and most complex role, and emphasize machines and precision metrics over general duties; a hiring manager scans fast.
Create a separate 'Certifications & Training' section if you have apprenticeships, tool-and-die licenses, or CNC programming certs; these often unlock ATS gates.

If you've worked with multiple industries (automotive, aerospace, medical), briefly call out industry-specific experience in bullet points—hiring managers often prioritize relevant vertical knowledge.
salarySignal: Entry-level machinists in the US typically earn $30,000–$40,000; experienced machinists and CNC specialists often reach $50,000–$70,000+, with tool-and-die makers and supervisory roles pushing into the $75,000–$90,000+ range.

faq.q: Should I put CNC programming languages on my machinist resume?
faq.a: Yes—absolutely. If you know G-code, M-code, or proprietary languages (Fanuc, Haas, Mazak), list them in your technical skills section. Many ATS systems search for these exact terms, and it's a quick signal of expertise.

faq.q: How do I describe hands-on experience if I'm coming from a trade apprenticeship?
faq.a: Treat your apprenticeship like a job: list the employer (shop or manufacturer), dates, and specific machines and projects. Use the same bullet-point format as regular work history, and highlight measurable outcomes or certifications earned.

faq.q: What if I mostly worked on older, manual machines—will that hurt my resume?
faq.a: No—manual machine skill is still valuable and shows foundational knowledge. Pair it with any CNC training or modern software you've picked up, and emphasize precision and problem-solving to show you can adapt to newer equipment.

faq.q: Do I need CAD experience to be competitive?
faq.a: It depends on the job and industry. Aerospace and medical device shops almost always want CAD fluency; general job shops may not. Check the posting—if CAD is mentioned, learn SolidWorks or AutoCAD basics and add it to your resume.

faq.q: How do I highlight safety and compliance without sounding generic?
faq.a: Be specific: mention OSHA certifications, lockout/tagout (LOTO) qualifications, or any safety audits you've led or participated in. For example: 'Achieved 2-year accident-free record' or 'Certified in OSHA 10-hour construction safety' show concrete commitment.
